Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: 3-Bucket System For $5,000 Monthly Income, For Early Retirees
data.description:
Introduction:

The S&P 500 (SP500) has been on a tear during the last 12–15 years. In fact, it has beaten most other investment strategies and funds during this period. To some extent, a large proportion of the gains of the S&P 500 have come from a handful of technology stocks, especially the so-called "Magnificent 7" stocks. Since the S&P 500 is a market-cap weighted index, it is currently overweighted in technology names. Will this winning streak of the S&P 500 of the past 10 years continue in the next 10 years? It is possible but highly unlikely. All that said, the S&P 500 has many positive aspects, especially for younger folks who are in their prime age and need to compound their capital for many years to come. These are the investors who should not care about the income aspect of the S&P 500, which is currently less than 1.2%. Also, they can tolerate the bumpy ride of the S&P 500 as they have a long time horizon.

However, if you are a retiree or someone who depends on a regular income stream from your investment portfolio, you can’t possibly survive on a 1.2% income yield, and you will be forced to sell shares often to raise the income. At the same time, the last thing you want during retirement is to worry about when to sell shares (or not to sell) to raise income for a living. If the market were going through some kind of correction, you may be forced to take big losses to raise income that you will probably never recover. It is easy to forget that at times, market corrections can last a couple of years (or more). One such example was after the dot-com bubble burst. Even when a correction lasts much shorter as we witnessed during the financial crisis, the stock prices may not recover to their pre-correction levels for many years to come.

In this article, we will present a balanced and highly diversified 3-bucket portfolio that is likely to provide 5% to 6% income and market-beating growth with lower volatility and less drawdowns. It will also aim to provide $5,000 on a monthly basis on a $1 million capital.

So, How Much Capital Would You need?

It depends on your goals and objectives. Do you plan to retire early? If so, you may need to plan for extra expenses in terms of funding your own medical insurance. For example, let’s say you plan to retire by the age of 55. Now, if your spouse plans to work much longer, you could obtain medical insurance at reasonable rates. But if you both, as a couple, are retiring early, then plan at least $1000-$1200 a month (or more) for medical insurance. That means keeping an additional amount of $120,000 to fund your medical insurance for the next 10 years. However, if you plan to retire at 65 when you become eligible to enroll in Medicare, you will still have Medicare premiums, but they will be much lower.

Other living expenses will also vary based on your personal situation. Is your house mortgage paid off? Will you still be supporting kids in college when you early retire? Do you live in a high-cost state? Can you downsize or move to a state where the cost of living is cheaper? So, these are some of the questions you may have to answer to be able to estimate your living expenses.

Once you know your annual living expenses, multiply them by a factor of 12 to 15 to calculate the capital you will need to retire (a factor of 15 is preferable to have some margin of safety).

Let’s work out by an example. Let’s say you are a 54-year-old and you want to retire early by next year at 55. After answering the above-stated questions, you figure out that you need $50,000 a year for your basic living expenses. Multiplying $50,000 by a factor of 15 (keeping a margin of safety), you should need $750,000 in retirement capital. Now, let’s add other expenses.

Basic amount of capital needed = $750,000.

Additional amount needed to fund medical insurance for the next 10 years = $120,000.

Add 2 years’ worth of living expenses (in a money-market account) = 2 x $60,000 = $120,000.

Total Capital needed = $750,000 + $120,000 + $120,000 = $990,000.

Let’s round off to $1 Million. This is what you require roughly to retire at the age of 55 based on your assumptions of living expenses. Sure, some folks can do it on much less, but this may be needed to maintain a comfortable living standard in most places. Sure, if you plan to do some part-time ‘gig’ during the early retirement period that results in income, this requirement of $1 million can be much lower.

Now, let’s see how well this amount of capital will be able to support you in your early retirement and subsequent full retirement journey. To calculate this, we will assume that one of the spouses would withdraw Social Security at the age of 65 (gets $2,000 a month at 65) and the second spouse would wait until 70 years of age (gets $3,000 per month at 70).

Table-1: Earnings and Withdrawals from Age 55-75, at 7.5% annual growth

Note: The living expenses are adjusted for inflation at 3% annually. The Social Security benefits are adjusted at 2.5% (for cost-of-living adjustment), but only after they start receiving them. The annualized growth rate of 7.5% (on a consistent basis) is taken, which is significantly lower than the market’s long-term returns.

Below, we will lay out an investment portfolio that should suit a large number of folks who are planning for a retirement portfolio. Obviously, not everyone will find it suitable for their individual needs or temperament. Nonetheless, we only try to provide a framework that can easily be modified or adjusted to suit specific needs.

Our portfolio will have four basic goals or objectives:

  • Moderate to High Income:

To anyone who wants to live off the income generated by their investment portfolio, they can’t do that with investments that yield 1% or 2%, unless they have a very large amount of capital saved. For most folks, who have $1 million or under, the portfolio needs to yield at least 5% but may be up to 6%. However, for folks who are not retired and have many years to go before retirement, the growth of the dividends may be more important than the current yield.

  • Growth of the capital:

Whether you plan to retire early or retire at the normal retirement age, the growth of capital is important at any stage. Inflation is never-ending and constant, and will always remain a fact of life. The long-term inflation in the U.S. has been between 2%-3% but at times much higher. So, when we talk about the total returns from any income investment, it should at least be 5% income yield plus the rate of inflation, preferably more.

  • Safety of the Capital:

The safety of your capital is important at any stage, but it certainly gains prominence after you have crossed a certain age. After retirement, it is even more critical. By safety, we also mean a lower level of volatility that the investor can tolerate without impacting their peace of mind. So, that’s why we believe that a small portion of the portfolio should utilize some kind of hedging mechanism. We will discuss this later in bucket-3. Besides, the rest of our portfolios will also be built around safer and diversified investments.

  • Maintenance and upkeep:

This is more of an individual choice. Some investors like to stay involved with their investments, while others prefer a hands-off approach as they do not have time or inclination. Folks who like a hands-off approach should focus on investment through broad-based funds rather than individual stocks.

Portfolio Structure:

We will have three components (or buckets) in this portfolio:

  • A Buy-and-Hold Bucket of Funds – 40% Allocation
  • A Buy-and-Hold DGI Stocks bucket: – 35% allocation
  • A Hedging/Rotation Bucket – 25% Allocation.

The first two buckets are of “buy-and-hold” nature and constitute 75% of the total capital. We are choosing this approach to ensure that the overall portfolio is very easy to implement and maintain going forward. These are also the “core” investments of this portfolio. Even though both buckets focus on dividends, the first one aims for higher income. The second bucket will consist of DGI stocks of companies that are large with stable sources of revenues, and also have substantial moats around their businesses. They also pay growing dividends, and we are aiming for an average of 4% plus income yield.

The third bucket (with 25% allocation) is a “Hedging” bucket. However, it is important to avoid the ongoing cost of hedging, as that would cut into total returns and add up over time. The best method for doing this without a cost is a Rotational method. That said, not everyone likes it because of the monthly trading involved. The monthly trading also takes away some shine if this is done in a taxable account. This method is best suited inside tax-deferred/sheltered accounts. Our goal for this bucket is to provide roughly 6% income and market-matching or market-exceeding growth.

(I) Bucket-1: A Bucket of Funds: 40% Allocation

This bucket will consist of a set of funds that focus on dividends and income. We will select seven funds that differ from one another in terms of the type of assets they hold, how they earn their income stream, or simply the types of asset classes. We believe 5 to 7 funds can provide enough diversification that we need. However, depending on personal preference, one can definitely go for more than seven funds. The seven funds selected by us provide an average of 7.58% income yield, assuming that they have equal allocation.

There are seven funds that we selected: (SCHD), (JEPI), (JEPQ), (AMLP), (FAGIX), (UTG), and (BIZD).

(II) Bucket-2: A DGI and Income Bucket of Individual Stocks: 35% Allocation

In this DGI bucket, we will select 15 individual stocks, selected on many different criteria. First, we want to cover as many different sectors or industry segments as possible. Secondly, we will try to include stocks that yield low income but are likely to provide high growth, and then some others that provide high current income but with less potential for growth.

Individual investors could definitely change or adjust based on their suitability. The idea here is to provide a template for a balanced portfolio to meet the goals of yield, growth, and safety.

These stocks/securities are: (JPM), (LYB), (BTI), (PEP), (LMT), (MPLX), (V), (MAIN), (UNH), (MAA), (UNP), (UPS), (O), (TXN), and (MSFT).

Table-3: Bucket of 15 stocks

Note: MPLX LP is a partnership and provides partnership income instead of dividends. Please be aware of the tax treatment.

(III) Bucket-3: Hedging Bucket (Rotation Bucket) – 25% Allocation

We admit, this rotational method is a bit complex and may not suit everyone. We specifically like it for income investors as it can provide a significant monthly income (usually 6%), in addition to providing hedging and market-beating growth. Three of the four securities selected provide high income (close to 8% to 10%), while the fourth one provides no income. Also, there will be times when a fund is replaced with the Treasury fund IEF (with much less income), but on average, the portfolio income will be 6% or more.

Rotation Method: The bucket is based on four securities, each being allocated 25%. Every month, we will calculate the total performance returns (including any distributions) over the previous three months and seven months for each of the four securities, as well as for the fallback security. We will assign 50% weightage to each of the two periods (3-month and 7-month performance) to calculate average performance. If the performance of any security is lower than the risk-free return provided by Cash (or 1-3 month Treasury), then it is replaced by a Treasury fund (IEF – the mid-term Treasury, in our example) for the next month; otherwise, it is retained. This process is repeated at the end of each month.

The four securities plus the fallback security are:

  1. (ADX) Adams Diversified Equity Fund

  3. (KYN) Kayne Anderson MLP Investment Closed Fund
  4. (QLD) ProShares Ultra QQQ

Risks:

There are always risks with stock investments. The portfolio described in this article has a high concentration of equity investments. The element of bonds or fixed income securities is relatively low. Although the portfolio employs a hedging strategy in the form of a Rotational bucket, it has limitations, and the allocation is only 25%. Also, since the fund employs both a fund bucket and an individual-stocks bucket, there may be some duplication for some of the stocks, even if it is minor. If we were to enter into a prolonged correction, this portfolio would lose value, and even the income may be impacted to some extent.

Concluding Thoughts:

The income of $5,000 per month (or $60,000 annually) is based on the assumption of a $1 million investment in the bucket portfolio. We have suggested the allocation of 37.5%, 37.5% and 25% to three buckets, respectively; however, the investor has the flexibility to adjust the allocation to suit their individual needs. The more conservative investors could also add a fourth bucket to hold some cash reserve or fixed deposits, though that may bring down the overall yield.

The bucket-based portfolio presented above should provide investors not only with a high income and high growth but also provide some protection against tough market conditions such as deep corrections and recessions.

data.title: Hold The Line: Lithium Argentina Balances Efficiency And Expansion Amid Lithium Downturn
data.description:
A structural opportunity in a depressed market

In an international context characterized by depressed lithium prices and a marked slowdown in the investment of new projects, Lithium Argentina (NYSE:LAR) offers a proposal that combines operational discipline, low-cost assets, next-generation technologies, and a scalable growth platform.

Unlike other producers, the company managed not only to sustain positive margins during Q1 2025 but also advance in its roadmap towards a potential annual production of more than 200,000 tons of lithium carbonate equivalent (LCE). To this is added a more favorable local regulatory environment with the implementation of RIGI in Argentina, which substantially improves the future profitability of large-scale mining projects.

Since its separation from Lithium Americas (LAC), the company I have previously analyzed, LAR’s strategy has been clear: to consolidate its position as a producer of ultra-low-cost brine lithium, leveraging strategic alliances and advanced technology.

Its most relevant operation, Cauchari-Olaroz, located in the province of Jujuy, has already reached an annualized production level of between 30,000 and 35,000 tons, with an effective cost of $6,634/ton sold according to Q1 2025 results. The company reported revenues of $58MM in that period, with a production of 7,200 tons, and reaffirmed its goal to meet the full production guide for the year.

The 44.8% economic participation in this project represents the current core of income for LAR, but does not exhaust its potential. The joint development of Pastos Grandes, Sal de la Puna, and Pozuelos, in partnership with Ganfeng (OTCPK:GNENY), sets out a growth roadmap that could bring its total capacity to more than 200,000 tons per year, using a modular approach and direct extraction (DLE) technologies.

Operational output and financial resilience

During Q1 2025, the Cauchari-Olaroz operation executed adjustment tasks, which included scheduled interruptions for maintenance and operational advances, both with the objective of improving lithium recovery and decreasing long-term costs. Although this meant operating below potential during Q1, already in April, the plant was operating above 85% of its capacity, marking a rapid recovery.

From LAR, they are optimistic and project that the H2 will bring even more solid results, in line with this trend.

Even in a scenario marked by weak prices, operating performance was solid: the average selling price stood at $8,085/ton, with a gross spread of ~$1,450/ton, a remarkable margin compared to larger but less efficient or integrated competitors. In addition, support capex was estimated at $600-$700/ton, confirming that the operation can remain profitable even in a low price environment.

DLE technology as a strategic expansion vector

One of the most relevant aspects of LAR’s strategy is its transition to direct extraction technologies, developed with Ganfeng. The company is implementing a demonstration plant of 5,000 tons per year, approved by the province of Jujuy, which will be integrated into the existing infrastructure. This technology uses solvent extraction (SX) combined with solar evaporation to achieve higher efficiency and less use of reagents.

The objective is not only to reduce costs but also to make production more flexible in order to better adapt to market conditions and global ESG requirements. This plant will also serve as a technological base for the expansion of Cauchari-Olaroz (Stage 2), which has already submitted its application to qualify under the regime of large investments RIGI, and for the future exploitation of regional projects Pozuelos-Pastos Grandes.

Sensitivity of share value to lithium price

In order to obtain the possible value of LAR action under different scenarios of lithium prices and operational margins, I performed a sensitivity analysis. I assumed an annual production of 32,000 tons and a 44.8% stake, in line with the company’s reporting. I applied a conservative multiple of 7x EV/EBITDA and 161 million outstanding shares, and I obtained the following table:

This analysis allows us to concretely visualize the operational leverage that LAR has: even in a depressed environment, the estimated fundamental floor is $1.50 per share, while with lithium prices recovering to levels of $12,000-$14,000 and solid margins, the value could be between $3.50 and $4.36. The current market price, around $2.90, already discounts the base scenario but doesn’t include the upside of future expansion or an improved macro environment.

Valuation by DCF

To complete the previous analysis, I developed a simplified DCF model based only on the current operation at Cauchari-Olaroz. I didn't include any expansion plans in order to estimate a fundamental valuation floor under conservative assumptions.

I assumed a steady output of 32,000 tonnes per year for 10 years, with an operating margin of 40% and a lithium price of $10,000/t. The estimated attributable EBITDA was $57.3 million. The sustaining capex was $22.4MM annually ($700/t) and included a working capital requirement of 10% of EBITDA. The discount rate used was 10 percent and assumed zero terminal growth.

The result was a net present value of approximately $445 MM, implying an estimated value per share of $2.77.

The DCF supports the thesis that the market already assigns a fair value to the current operation but does not incorporate the potential of projects in the development stage, the impact of the RIGI regime, or an eventual improvement in lithium prices.

Conclusions

LAR combines three rare elements together: proven low operating cost, concrete growth pipeline, and state-of-the-art extraction technology. All this in a more stable and favourable regulatory environment than was the case until recently.

The risks, of course, are not minor: weak lithium prices, exposure to the Argentine macroeconomic context, and the need to finance growth. However, even in that scenario, the downside seems limited, while the upside potential remains.

For all this, the best option in this scenario is to hold the action and analyze the performance of the sector in the coming months.

data.title: Drill, Baby, Drill: 9 Stocks With Enough Firepower To Outperform For Decades
data.description:
Introduction

One of the worst things about investing is just how important patience is.

Making money requires a lot of patience, as explained by the Rule of 72.

Essentially, it's a quick way to estimate how long it takes for our capital to double based on different expected returns. We simply divided 72 by the expected return.

As we can see below, it takes 72 years to double one's capital with a 1% return. Based on a 10% return, that number drops to 7.2 years.

The problem is that patience isn't one of our strengths. In recent years, this has only gotten worse, as discussed by Psychology Today:

Four seconds. That's how long we'll wait for a website to load before irritation sets in. By 10 seconds, most of us have already bailed.

96%. That's how many Americans admitted to knowingly consuming extremely hot food or drinks that burn their mouths, showing a widespread willingness to risk personal discomfort due to impatience.

One minute (or less). That's how long most of us are willing to be on hold before we hang up the phone. - Psychology Today

This may explain why the average holding periods of stocks have dropped to less than one year, as we can see in the chart below, using WFE and IMF data.

Personally, I'm very patient when it comes to investing, as I usually buy stocks with a "never sell" mindset, which means, regardless of what happens on the stock market, the last thing that comes to my mind is selling investments.

However, it's not always easy, especially because I like to buy stocks in unloved sectors to improve my risk/reward.

Energy is one of these sectors, which is why I have written countless articles on this subject, including one that carries the title "My Favorite Place To Buy Stocks - And It's Not Even Close."

This is what I wrote back then:

I'm convinced my higher-for-longer inflation thesis is well on track.

It's the silent killer that's our purchasing power. And with secular factors unfolding, I don't see a sustainable path to the Fed's 2% target.

To make matters worse, tariffs and a weakening dollar are adding fuel to the inflation fire, as they set the stage for stubbornly high growth in prices and a potential second wave of inflation, although that would require an upswing in global cyclical growth, which President Trump and Bessent are working on.

That's why I'm doubling down on energy, one of the few sectors trading below historical valuations while it offers both yield and inflation-beating potential.

So, despite OPEC's unusual (to put it mildly) supply moves and cyclical headwinds, the long-term setup is compelling, supported by dwindling U.S. shale growth, tight inventories, and a dollar slump that should support commodities with a lagging effect.

The problem with these theses, and any thesis, in general, is that it takes time to unfold. Macroeconomic developments are slow, and market rotations need a while to heat up.

In 2020/2021, the same applied when investors thought value stocks were dead. It took roughly a year after the first lockdowns for inflation to rise and capital to rotate.

While 2020 and 2021 were horrible for value investors, the patient were rewarded handsomely.

Now, I expect the same to happen again.

Having said all of this, I am using this article to do at least two things:

  1. I am updating my thesis based on new findings in the U.S.
  2. I am presenting a lot more food for thought in the form of actionable ideas.

It's All About Reserves

Generally speaking, investing in basic materials and energy is all about identifying the best reserves. After all, since their entire businesses depend on adding value by extracting commodities from the ground, the biggest bottlenecks tend to be their ability to do just that.

In America, this is a huge deal, as the decline of Tier 1 reserves for oil and gas companies has caused the shale revolution to end. Currently, the mighty Permian Basin is the only basin capable of output growth, as other spots like Bakken and Eagle Ford have lost all momentum since the pandemic.

As the latest data, which was released earlier this month, shows, 2023 saw an overall decline in reserves, as proved oil reserves fell by 4%, while natural gas reserves declined by 13%.

Most oil reserves are located in Texas, with New Mexico coming in second, as the Permian Basin covers both states through the Delaware Sub-Basin.

Texas is also home to the most natural gas reserves, followed by Pennsylvania, the heart of the Marcellus Basin.

While there is no doubt that the best spots have been drilled, it's way too early to panic, as Mizuho just came out and made the case that there are at least 25 more years of oil shale wells left to drill in the United States.

According to their report, there are roughly 269,000 wells left to drill, compared to 290,000 horizontal wells that have been drilled in core basins since 2008. Back then, the shale revolution started its monumental journey.

Even at very subdued oil prices, the form estimates oil production can remain flat until 2033.

“Using the adjusted well productivity for remaining well inventory, we estimate U.S. shale can hold Lower 48 oil volumes flat for about nine years—or until 2033—below $60/bbl WTI,” he wrote.

As for U.S. shale gas, he expects remaining inventory in Appalachia, the Haynesville and other gas-weighted acreage “could sustain their share of U.S. natural gas volumes for approximately 22 years below $3.50/Mcf at the wellhead.” - Hart Energy

In other words, we may be at a sweet spot, where "we" are running out of Tier 1 resources fast enough to cause the supply side to be more favorable for higher oil prices without the risks of running so fast out of reserves that it becomes impossible to find good energy stocks.

That's what the next part of this article is about.

When Reserves & Valuation Tailwinds Meet

On top of educating us on oil and gas reserves, Mizuho also explained which companies have the best reserves, saving me a lot of work.

On the Permian basin, these four companies stand out:

  • Diamondback Energy (FANG): 25 years of reserves
  • Coterra Energy (CTRA): 23 years
  • Occidental Petroleum (OXY): 22 years
  • Chevron (CVX): 22 years

Just one of these companies has beaten the Energy ETF (XLE) over the past five years.

Needless to say, these companies don't cease to exist after 22-25 years. All this means is that their best reserves are expected to last this long without any new discoveries. Especially at higher oil prices, their profitable reserves increase. Furthermore, Chevron has a lot of non-Permian operations, which means the actual number for the entire Chevron Corporation is higher.

In fact, Chevron, being a supermajor, is almost like an oil ETF itself.

The following natural gas-focused companies were highlighted by Mizuho to have the best reserve profiles, each with at least 15 years of core reserves:

  • EOG Resources (EOG)
  • Range Resources (RRC)
  • Antero Resources (AR)
  • EQT Corp. (EQT)

All of these picks have beaten the Energy ETF XLE over the past five years.

Then there's Crescent Energy (CRGY), another inventory leader. However, it mainly operates in the Texas Eagle Ford Basin, which is why I list it separately from the others, as these were Permian oil stocks.

The reason all natural gas picks beat the XLE ETF and just one of these oil companies is that natural gas stocks were punished much more than oil during (and prior to) the pandemic.

Personally, I would give each of these stocks a Buy rating, as I am familiar with their business models. However, I want to add some more emphasis on these stocks, as each of these brings something special to the table.

Needless to say, as this is a multi-ticker article, I cannot give each pick the attention it deserves, which is why I will continue to bring up my favorite plays in future articles.

Diamondback Energy, for example, is one of America's premier drillers in the Permian. It's the result of aggressive M&A since the Great Financial Crisis, which turned a great idea into one of the most efficient drillers in all of North America.

Currently yielding around 4%, it returns 50% of its free cash flow to shareholders. As it reduces acquisition debt, that percentage will increase to 75% over the next few years. Its dividend potential is significant, with double-digit free cash flow yields even at $70 WTI.

Coterra has most of its assets in Culberson County, a major part of the Permian Basin. The company, which generates most of its income from natural gas, is benefiting from better takeaway capacity (relative pricing) and has returned 89% of its free cash flow last year to shareholders.

Currently yielding 3.8%, I think CTRA's buybacks and dividend strategy make it a great total return play in the Texas natural gas space.

Occidental has made a lot of headlines due to the involvement of Warren Buffett. I have not covered it a lot, as the company is currently trying to find its way, as it is neither a high-yield dividend payer nor a company known for exceptional performance, as it is balancing oil and gas production, midstream operations, petrochemicals, and carbon capture.

What makes this company stand out to me is the fact that it trades at less than 4x operating cash flow, a mile below the 8-10x multiples I usually apply to high-quality oil companies. Its own longer-term average is roughly 7.0x operating cash flow.

Hence, OXY is one of the companies I expect to double in the years ahead if we get some support from higher commodity prices and improved operating results.

With regard to EOG Resources, if there's one thing the company is known for, it's the ability to consistently find new places to drill, using very efficient methods that have allowed it to grow its dividend twice as fast as its average peer since 2019.

Currently yielding 3.5%, the company hasn't cut its dividend in almost 30 years and has a bigger focus on natural gas close to high-margin export facilities on the Gulf Coast.

Especially at subdued valuations, management is eager to use buybacks to improve the per-share value of the business, which I expect to pay off handsomely in the years ahead, both for its stock price and future dividend growth, as buybacks reduce total dividend payments, leaving more room for future dividend growth.

Antero Resources and Range Resources are my two favorite natural gas producers in the U.S., as both focus on ultra-high-margin operations in the Marcellus Basin. Antero has more than 20 years' worth of core reserves, while that number for Range is north of 30 years.

Even better, the overwhelming majority of these reserves are profitable below $2.00 Henry Hub, which means both companies can still drill profitably when others are forced to cut their output. This adds stability, although both have very volatile stock prices.

If I were running an energy portfolio, I would own both.

On a side note, I own Antero Midstream (AM), which owns the processing and gathering assets of Antero Resources. The strong relationship with Antero Resources and its operations was a major reason why I made the investment.

Then there's EQT.

EQT also operates in the Marcellus Basin, where it enjoys three decades of core reserves and a strong integrated portfolio of midstream assets, which have allowed it to become a free cash flow leader in its industry.

Last but not least, I have never covered Crescent Energy, which was a big mistake, as I think it's a great gem.

The company is a major producer in the Texas Eagle Ford Basin, which is why I did not put it in the same basket as the Permian players in this article. In this area, it is the third-largest producer that has more than tripled its footprint over the past two years.

Currently, it has an inventory life of at least 15 years without the need to spend any additional capital.

Moreover, because of its low valuation (the stock price influences ratios like free cash flow yield) and efficient operations, it has one of the highest free cash flow yields of the entire industry.

Currently yielding nearly 6.0%, the company aims to both grow the dividend over time and use buybacks for additional (indirect) capital returns.

If I did not run such a concentrated portfolio of just 16 stocks with already elevated energy exposure, I would likely buy a few shares at these levels.

Takeaway

Patience in investing can be brutal. However, essential, especially in unloved sectors like energy, where macro trends take time to unfold.

I would be lying if I said my "never sell" mindset is never tested when waiting for these ideas to play out. Luckily, history tends to reward those who hold and do their homework.

Right now, U.S. energy offers a sweet spot, as dwindling Tier 1 reserves tighten supply, yet companies like Diamondback, Coterra, EQT, and others boast decades of inventory, often paired with deep value and fat yields.

While the Permian and Marcellus basins are goldmines, the real edge comes from buying when others won't.

Whether it's Occidental's dirt-cheap cash flow or Antero's $2.00-proof gas reserves, the setup screams opportunity.

To me, energy isn't a quick trade. It's a slow burn.

For those willing to wait, the payoff could be explosive.

That's why I will continue to focus on this area.

Risks To My Thesis

The biggest risk is a further decline in oil and gas prices. While this would eventually force a more favorable supply/demand balance, it would be bearish for every company I just mentioned.

Moreover, energy stocks tend to be volatile. Please be aware of this and do your own due diligence before buying any of the companies on my list.
